nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reefs and drop-offs exposed to strong tidal currents; depth range approximately 5–30 m. Juveniles tend to occupy deep rubble slopes with invertebrate growth.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on sponges and tunicates in the wild, with algae and occasional invertebrates also consumed. In captivity, tends to accept Spirulina, marine algae, sponge-enriched angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, krill, and other meaty fare. Generally advised to offer varied meals multiple times daily; newly acquired specimens have been known to initially refuse prepared foods and may benefit from live brine shrimp or sponge-encrusted rock to trigger feeding.

summary: The Black Velvet Angelfish (Chaetodontoplus melanosoma) is an Indo-Pacific species with a velvety black body, gray-splashed head, and yellow tail; generally considered intermediate in difficulty and has been known to nip at corals and clam mantles.
careNotes:
The Black Velvet Angelfish is generally considered one of the more manageable large angelfishes, though it tends to be shy and sensitive during initial acclimation. Newly introduced specimens have been known to hide and refuse food; a well-established aquarium with ample live rock for grazing and numerous hiding places tends to improve outcomes. Keeping this species singly is generally advised, as conspecific and congeneric aggression has been reported, particularly among juveniles.

Because this species has been documented eating sponges and tunicates in the wild, sponge-enriched angelfish preparations are broadly recommended once the fish is settled. The Black Velvet Angelfish is generally considered not reef safe — it has been known to nip at stony and soft corals, clam mantles, and tubeworms, particularly when underfed. Like many marine angelfishes, it is susceptible to marine ich when stressed.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to be hermaphroditic with no reliable external sex differences between male and female. Breeding in captivity is considered very difficult and has rarely been documented; spawning attempts are not commonly reported in the hobby literature.

Order confirmed as Acanthuriformes.
